speaker: interviewer
text: --in the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was saved by the kapo Schmidt. When Chaim Kaminski told her that I have been-- I put--you know, my number has been taken to be put to gas chambers, she-- you know, as I said before, was a very influential woman. And she called me over and said she's going to get me out, get my numbers, you know, out of the list. And two weeks later, they made what they call a [GERMAN], which means nobody was allowed to go out of the barracks they always let the siren sounds for the [GERMAN], which was a horrible sound. And they came to get the people whose numbers were written down. There were 2,000 women whose numbers were written down. And every time they said [GERMAN], which is 48,000, I was laying on the-- on the-- on the bed. You can't call it bed. On the [POLISH]. And mattress-- under the mattress. And I was shaking. And my mother was sitting on top of me because I was shaking. I got malaria from-- from fear. And somehow or other, they didn't take me, because she did-- you know, got my name out of the list. Schmidt, the kapo. And that night, the lorries came and they took 2,000 women naked to the gas chambers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, we had a perfect view of the gas chambers with the big chimney, because it was very close. The stench of the bodies that were being burned, and there was not smoke coming out of the chimney, but fire. That's how they were burning. Gassing-- first they gassed and burned the 2,000 women. Young women. They were all young women because the old person didn't get to go to Birkenau to start with. I went to the Schmidt that following night, or the same night. Following night. To thank her for saving my life. And she-- I gave her such a speech in Czechoslovakian that she was a very hardened woman, you know. But she cried. And she said to me, you can come back to work for me. I-- I have forgiven you. And I worked for her. And then one day, again one Saturday-- I have to bring this up because this is a very important thing. There was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because they say that the Jewish people went to-- to-- to death like lambs to slaughter, I have to bring this up. One Saturday, there was again the siren sounding and [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. And all of a sudden, we're all in the block. That was during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, we heard shooting and shooting and shooting. And heard terrible explosions. And what happened was that the Sonderkommandos-- which only consisted of Jewish men-- you know what Sonderkommandos was? They exploded a crematorium. They made a revolt, an uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: They cut the wires, and they all ran away. They all ran away. And-- and I think one crematorium exploded in-- in the-- the-- the one that was used mostly. The one the closest, I-- I think, to the camp, was exploded, you know. And unfortunately, we heard later on that they were already captured.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Chaim Kaminski?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Chaim Kaminski was shot. That's how Chaim Kaminski lost his life. He was shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was part of the revolt?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in the-- pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was part of the revolt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. Yes. He was part of the revolt. Yes. That's-- he-- he was a kapo there. He must have been the-- the-- the main person in the revolt or the uprising. And after that, we were still in camp. And I forgot to tell you that while I was working in camp, how this-- this uprising came all about was there was a young woman working in the [GERMAN] whose name was Roza Robota. And this Roza Robota with three other girls were smuggling dynamite to the men. That's how the uprising came all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: This girls were-- it was a whole operation. They were taking the dynamite from a Jewish woman who were working in ammunition factory. And it took them maybe 12 months. Little by little, they smuggled the powder, the dynamite, to the men. Powder by powder, you know? It took them a long time. In the hems of their frocks. Everywhere, you know? And Roza Robota did this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Roza-- Roza Robota and three other girls. One was Alla Gartner. The other two girls' names, I don't know. There was a whole lot of people involved in it. And that Roza Robota was caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a friend of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were working together in [GERMAN]. Although she was older than I was, we became very friendly. She was a-- a-- a very lovely girl. She was in her maybe 20. Very idealistic. And a great Zionist. And she was always telling me that how if she ever survives, she will live in Palestine, which was Palestine then, not Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was telling me always, you will see one day, we'll pay them back. We will pay them back. We'll do something. We'll do something. She always used to tell me that, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the uprising. I wanted to mention that. And after that, they were-- we were sent again to another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with Roza?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the camp-- and the camp was called family camp. Still in Birkenau. And one day, the camp-- they came and they said that every girl in the camp has to give a liter blood to be sent to the-- to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is, you know, really and truly the irony of it is that they-- you know what [GERMAN] is? They consider us so inferior that we were not allowed to talk to a German person, let alone marry. We were completely [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] inferior. We were not-- subhuman. But our blood was good for the German soldiers, you see? So they came to the barrack. At the time, I was-- my mother and I were sharing a barrack with a lovely Yugoslav Jewish girl. She was 18 years old. And they came. And my mother, we were-- hid, and I was-- I also hid.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they found me. They didn't find my mother, lucky. But they found me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I-- somewhere, you know, in some stupid place. Where could you hide in a barrack? And they got me. And when they took me to the hospital to give blood, there was a doctor sitting there and a Jewish nurse. And this nurse took one look at me and she said to the doctor, please. She-- she begged him to save me, not to make me give blood. Because I can't survive it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was begging him so long that he said, OK, let her go. So I went. But this Yugoslav girl who was already 18, she gave blood. And two days later, she was dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- Just to return to Roza Robota and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to return to Roza Robota. What-- what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to her was like that. They were found. You know, they must have tortured the men and the men told them how they got the dynamite. And they got-- they-- they caught Roza Robota. I was going to tell you, because later on I decided-- they were taking people to got-- to go to Auschwitz. And Auschwitz was a [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a show-- a show camp. Whenever they had Red Cross or somebody to show, that's was the lager. Because there, they-- they tried to keep the people look semi-normal, you know? And not to be like-- we were all, you know--I was a Muselmann. You know what a Muselmann is? Because I had typhus in Auschwitz-- in-- in-- in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you were a Muselmann in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a Muselmann. Man

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain what that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A Muselmann was a person who was a skeleton, and who really had no hope to live. And I had typhus, and I became a Muselmann. But somehow or other, because of that kapo Schmidt, I didn't have to go to the [GERMAN], the-- she saw to it that somebody came to have a look. And she also send me food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that way, I came a little bit. But that's-- soon after I was put to gas chambers because I was a Muselmann, you know? I understand. So Roza Robota, what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Roza Robota was-- I wanted to go to Auschwitz. I'll come to Roza Robota. I wanted to go to Auschwitz, and my mother and I, we went to Auschwitz. And there, one day they put us all in one place. And we hear that Roza Robota was captured together with the three other girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Roza Robota was tortured in the most inhuman way. She never gave a name of anybody except one person who was already dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You understand? And we were all put on this big, you know, place. And they had four gallows. Gallows, yeah? That's what you call where they hang people? And they brought those four girls, and they hang them. And we had to stand there and watch. And I averted my head. And an SS woman noticed that. So she came over and slapped me on the face. And she said, you watch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I watched. I had to. I looked. And as they were putting the noose on her neck, she said in Polish, 'Revenge.' But she didn't say the whole name-- word 'revenge.' In Polish, it's [POLISH]. She said 'zemst--' and we knew what she meant. She was saying, avenge me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, the Russians started bombing Auschwitz. And you know, the front was coming closer. And the Germans-- the sirens, the planes were going all the time. And really, we were laying there on our beds. I keep-- I keep referring to it as bed, because I know that you don't understand what [POLISH] means. [POLISH] is a place where we were sleeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in that place where we were sleeping, my mother and I during the night and at-- during the day the girls were working. At night, and was sleeping in the same bed. And we were laying there. And we were laughing when the bombs were falling, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Germans, the guards, you have no idea how they crumble like-- like nothing before our very eyes. Those big powerful bullies, that-- that-- that we were so petrified of. The minute the bombs started falling, they became in-- they were reduced to nothing. They were shivering and shaking in their boots. Anyway, they couldn't part with us, obviously. So they started evacuating us. And they evacuated us to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we were on the march. I suppose you call it death march. At the time, I didn't know it was called death march. Well, we started-- they gave us each a blanket and a tin of meat and a loaf of breads. And we started marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my mother. I was with my mother all the time till the end. And we were marching. And we march for three days. And we did 30 kilometers a day. And there were SS persons like guards with big dogs. And if you couldn't walk, they shoot you or they put-- the dogs know-- knew how to tear the Jewish people apart. They were tending-- tearing them apart. Well, the first thing we got rid of was the blankets. Both blankets. Because we could see we can't walk doing 30 kilometers an hour carrying things. So we got rid of the blankets. It was winter time. And then we open one tin of meat. And it was pure salt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we threw out the meat. Because we thought to ourselves, if we eat the meat we'll die of thirst. And there was no water. There was snow laying around. And we ate the snow. So we already left the bread. And we were marching. And at nighttime, they let us sleep, either outside or in a barn. At one night, I slept next to a cow. And I was petrified the whole night, you know? In a barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of women were very clever, and they hid and they didn't come out anymore. And soon after the Russians came, and they were liberated. But my mother and I were stupid, and we marched. And they put us on trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know how many women were on the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands and thousands of women. I wouldn't have a clue how many. The whole of Birkenau and Auschwitz. I wouldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --have a clue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you walk through towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see local people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked through towns, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were just like a piece of rubbish. Nobody took notice of us. You know? We walked-- first we walked through Polish towns. And then walked through probably German towns too. Nobody took any notice. We were just a-- a piece of rubbish.

speaker: interviewer
text: That man who was in charge, he had a very high rank in the army, the SS man. When he got to the trains, he looked at all of us. And he said in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What should I do with this old piece of shit? Meaning us.

speaker: interviewee
text: What should I do with all this, meaning us. See, we heard it, my mother and I, because we were close to him. We heard him saying that. And they put us in trains again. This time, the trains were-- had no roofs. They were half carriages. A real cattle train. And it was-- [SIGHS] must has been either December or January. It was very, very cold. And the snow was falling on-- on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was after the three days of marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was 1945, January?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1945. After-- after this three days of marching and witnessing the most, you know, terrible atrocities and killings and-- and-- and-- and tearing people to pieces. You only had to be-- be unable to-- to walk one step forward and they-- and they shot you, you know? I had to-- when they looked at you, you had to march like a-- like a soldier, to show them how healthy you are. And they put us on those cattle trains. And the snow was snowing on us. And-- day and night. I don't know how long we were in there. And we couldn't go to the toilet. But we had an empty tin. And we used to throw the things out of the cattle train, of the carriage. And a couple of woman, they stopped the train a few times. And if-- if you went down, they didn't let you up. They shoot-- shot quite a few people. So we-- we didn't go down anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had anything-- did you have anything to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a bread. We had-- well, I said a bread. We had a piece of bread, my mother and I. And we had a few cigarettes. You see, cigarettes was like a-- like money in camp, because you could change the cigarettes for food. People who were smoking would give away their food for cigarettes. And my mother and I, when we had a lot of food still in-- in Birkenau, we change it for a few cigarettes. And so we had a-- a bit of bread and those few cigarettes. This was our security, those few cigarettes. Anyway, we arrived in a camp. They stopped at a camp. I think it was Buchenwald. It was a big, big man's camp. We were on our way to Bergen-Belsen. I really don't remember what camp it was. Was huge camp. And the men that came to the train, they were walking corpses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've never seen really walking-- like you see in the-- if you have seen documentary of the-- of the people that were in camp, that's what they look like. And my mother took such a pity on one man. They were Polish Jews, they were talking to us. She took such a terrible pity that she took our bread and she gave it to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to her, Mama, I said, what have you done? Now we have nothing to eat. You should have given him half, and you should have kept the half for us. And my mother said to me, don't you worry, Lusia, she said to me. Because God will help us to have bread. We will get somehow food. Don't worry, she said. And soon enough from the next carriage somebody said, is anybody here who's got cigarettes? We want cigarettes for bread. So we gave them the cigarettes, and we had some bread. And then we arrived in Bergen-Belsen. I don't know how long we were traveling for.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this time, we had a very nice man on the-- SS man on the-- in the carriage with us. He-- he was-- we all decided that he must be Jewish. We-- we really did, because he was so kind to us. And he only wanted the girls-- there was a girl who used to sing beautifully there in the carriage. In-- in every situation, there was always a girl who used to sing.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Birkenau, there was a girl who used to sing beautiful Kol Nidre when there was Yom Kippur, you know? There was always a girl with a beautiful voice who would all of a sudden sing. And there was a girl who sang in this carriage, and he wanted only Jewish songs to-- to-- ask her only to sing Jewish song, [HEBREW]-- all kind of Jewish songs, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was so kind. He gave away his food to the girls. So we decided he was Jewish. Anyway, and we arrived in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived, we arrived in Celle. The little township was called Celle. We went through Hanover, and we arrived in Celle. It was a little township. That's where the train stopped. And we were told we're going to Bergen-Belsen. We marched from Celle to Bergen-Belsen. It was about five, six kilometers hourly. And the thing after the war, the people in Celle never knew there was such a huge camp like Bergen-Belsen. The Germans. Because they didn't-- they wanted to whitewash themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said, we never knew there was a camp, which is a lot of rubbish because transports were coming continuously, and they continuously saw people marching. And they knew. How, five, six kilometers away, how didn't you know there was a camp? But this is beside the point.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got to Bergen-Belsen. And Bergen-Belsen was the most horrible camp. Oh, they all-- were all horrible. There, really we were starving, my mother and I. Absolutely starving. Everybody was starving. And you-- you were sleeping on the floor just like in Majdanek, we also sleeping on the-- on the concrete floor without blankets. And that's how we were sleeping in-- in the beginning in Majdanek, the first few weeks. And in-- in Bergen-Belsen we were also sleeping on-- on a floor. And when you woke up in the morning, there were dead bodies laying next to you. People were just dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything happen to you when you arrived at Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you what, we were so numb, that I don't even remember. We-- we were really numb, you know, when we-- with-- with everything that happen. That we just marched in and were given a-- a block, and we were laying down on the-- on the floor, and that was it. And we were starving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm coming to that now. A woman said to me, you know, that Schmidt, the kapo from [GERMAN], she was-- originally they sent her to Ravensbruck. But you know, the [GERMAN] name was Kramer. He was the [GERMAN], I think, in Birkenau too. And in Bergen-Belsen. He-- yes, he was a [GERMAN] in Birkenau too. He was hanged later on in Poland, I think, or-- after Nuremberg Trial he was hanged. And he needed her for the [GERMAN] in-- in Bergen-Belsen. Bergen-Belsen was a huge camp. A huge camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he sent for her two SS-- she came with a normal train, you know, like people travel. Not a-- not a cattle train. Just with two SS. And the woman said to me, she's here now. Why don't you go and see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found out in which block she is. And I went to see her. And when she saw me, she started crying. And she said to me, ah, she used to always call me [GERMAN], my little heart. And she said, you are here. And she started to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said to me, I want you to come and work for me in [GERMAN]-- in [GERMAN] again. And bring your mother too. Is your mother alive? I said yes. Bring your mother too. And my mother and I worked in [GERMAN] in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, you couldn't sell anything. But what keeped us-- kept us alive was that every day at 12 o'clock, she would call [GERMAN], which mean messenger girl. And I would go to her room. And she will hand me a sandwich, a two slices of bread with bacon in it. And my mother and I shared that. And that's how we survived Bergen-Belsen. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was three months in Bergen-Belsen. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the other SS in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen, no, I don't. But there-- I remembers the SS in-- in-- in Birkenau. There was somebody called Hessler who you-- we used to call him [GERMAN], the liar. Because he was approachable. You could approach him and ask for something. And he'll promise you that he's going to give it to you or do it for you. And he never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to call him liar. And one day when I was still in Birkenau-- I have to tell you that. I told you it won't be chronicle-- chronicle order. But I have to tell you-- I feel I have to tell you that one day when I was still in Birkenau, the transport from Lodz started coming. And my auntie, my mother's sister, came with her two daughters. Her son and her husband must have been separated. And a woman came with regards from my auntie to say that she's very hungry. She was already from-- in a different lager, but also in-- in Birkenau. And-- and she's very cold. So immediately, you know, because I worked the [GERMAN], we sent through the woman two jumpers-- or three jumpers for the-- and bread for-- for my cousins, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she received it because we got a letter from her. The woman came again. And she said, your auntie with your cousin has been put to gas. Your other cousin's all right. And she-- and I was walking in the camp and I saw the [GERMAN]. I think her name was Mendel. And I went over to her. You were not allowed to speak directly to any SS personnel, because you were rubbish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I went to her. And I said to her, look-- in German. By that time, I could speak German. I said to her, look.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't tell her my auntie came. I said, do you know-- I said, my mother and my sister came from Lodz on a transport and they have been put to gas chambers. I said, please, would you save them. My mother and my sister, please would you save them? So she put her hand on the gun. And she said to me in German, see that you vanish because if you don't vanish, I'm going to kill you. So I went because I knew that she was going to do that. So that's why I went back for you to know that you-- that's how my auntie perished in Auschwitz with my cousin. She was 18 years old. She would not let her mother go on her own. How my father died, I don't know, because I never heard from him again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- In Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bergen-Belsen, how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen, as I said to you, people were dying left and center. And that was really the end. I think it really was the end except that my mother and I were lucky to have this position in Bergen-Belsen, and we had food. And we were not the worst off. And we were only there three months. And the Germans-- all of a sudden, the Germans put on white bands. And there was a talk that the front is coming close. There was all the time rumors that we are going to be soon either liberated or killed. Something will happen because the German are losing the war. We knew that the Germans are losing the war. And they took girls and they started digging around the camp. And we know that they are putting mines, because they put mines around Buchenwald. They were ready to put-- to blow it up when the-- when the whats-the-name came. When the liberation came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we thought they're going to do the same in Bergen-Belsen. But they never managed to do-- to do anything because they were wearing white bands. And one day they all vanished. They just-- just went. And all of a sudden, we found ourselves without SS, and all the [GERMAN] went with them. When I said [GERMAN], the kapos, the Lageraltestes, you know? The people who had big jobs there. Because they probably would-- were scared that they would be, you know, punished from the prisoners later on-- What about Schmidt?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why I'm bringing it up. She went too. And she has never done anybody any harm, but she went too. And I was searching for her, looking for her, because I really wanted to help her. I wanted to save her life now. But I couldn't find her anymore. And I never, ever heard of her again. So one day--
